Client 2: And for me, I want to know we’re okay month to month. I don’t love surprises.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Roughly, what’s your household income in a year—everything coming in combined?
Client 1: Combined, our gross is 74550 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month to run the household?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—2850 a month, give or take a little depending on travel.

Client 1: Combined, our gross is 74550 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month to run the household?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—2850 a month, give or take a little depending on travel.
Advisor: Okay. And any debt payments inside that—mortgage, car loan, credit cards—anything with a required monthly payment?
Client 1: No, monthly debt payments are 0. No mortgage, no car note.

Client 2: And for me, I want to know we’re okay month to month. I don’t love surprises.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Roughly, what’s your household income in a year—everything coming in combined?
Client 1: Combined, our gross is 74550 a year.

Client 2: We track it pretty closely—2850 a month, give or take a little depending on travel.

Client 1: No, monthly debt payments are 0. No mortgage, no car note.
Advisor: Great. One sec—just writing that down. Now, zooming out to the bigger picture: about how much do you have in investable accounts total, not counting the house?
Client 1: Investable assets total are 542400.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll break that into the actual accounts so we’re not hand-waving. Alex, what are the main buckets you’re thinking of when you say that total?
Client 1: There’s a taxable account on an advisor platform—294400.

Client 2: I do—there’s an account at a brokerage, basically bank-type money, and it’s 67200.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And then separate from investable accounts, you also have your home. What are you valuing the primary residence at right now?
Client 1: We have it at 171900.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m clear, you’re not carrying any loans against it—no HELOC or anything like that?
Client 1: Correct, none.

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month to run the household?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—2850 a month, give or take a little depending on travel.

Advisor: Perfect. Now, switching gears a bit—how are you engaging with an advisor today? Like, are we talking a brokerage rep, an insurance agent, or an independent advisory firm?
Client 2: I’m not sure what the label is—like, are you a broker?
Advisor: Good question. I’m not a broker. The channel here is an independent advisory firm—an RIA, meaning a registered investment advisor. Does that match what you were expecting?
Client 1: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great, thanks. Let me just jot that down.

Client 1: Yes, there’s a 401k IRA at a bank—149050.
Client 2: Wait—when you say “at a bank,” you mean it’s held there, right? Not like a checking account.
Advisor: Exactly—held there, not a checking account. Thanks for flagging that, Jennifer. Any other investments, Alex—maybe something less liquid?
Client 1: We also have private markets on the advisor platform—31800.
Advisor: Okay. Jennifer, do you have any accounts in your name we should include in that investable total?

Advisor: Hi Alex, hi Jennifer—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d say just getting organized—what we have, what’s coming in, and making sure we’re not missing something obvious.
Client 2: And for me, I want to know we’re okay month to month. I don’t love surprises.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Roughly, what’s your household income in a year—everything coming in combined?

Advisor: Hi Alex, hi Jennifer—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make today’s meeting feel like a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d say just getting organized—what we have, what’s coming in, and making sure we’re not missing something obvious.

Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Roughly, what’s your household income in a year—everything coming in combined?

Advisor: Yeah, exactly—the bracket percent band you’re typically in.
Client 1: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And then risk—when markets swing around, how would you describe your overall comfort level? Conservative, moderate, aggressive—plain English is fine.
Client 2: I’m more on the cautious side, but not extreme.
Client 1: Yeah, overall we’re moderate.

Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not missing anyone—no other adults living with you full-time, like an adult child or a parent?
Client 1: No, it’s just us—2 adults.
Advisor: Okay, thanks. Now I want to grab a couple more household basics—then we’ll move into income and the investment accounts. First: Alex and Jennifer, are you two married?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Advisor: Great. And do either of you have any dependents you’re financially responsible for—kids, grandkids, anyone like that?

Client 2: And for me, I want to know we’re okay month to month. I don’t love surprises.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with the cash-flow basics, then we’ll map the accounts and any insurance. Roughly, what’s your household income in a year—everything coming in combined?

Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do you two typically spend per month to run the household?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—2850 a month, give or take a little depending on travel.

Client 2: I always mix this up—are you asking the bracket percent?
Advisor: Yeah, exactly—the bracket percent band you’re typically in.
Client 1: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And then risk—when markets swing around, how would you describe your overall comfort level? Conservative, moderate, aggressive—plain English is fine.
Client 2: I’m more on the cautious side, but not extreme.

Client 1: Retired, yeah.
Advisor: Okay. Alex, one more detail on income—earlier we captured the rental income separately, but I also want your overall gross annual income number as you think of it. What should I use there?
Client 1: I always think of it as about fifty-eight thousand a year—hang on—let me say it exactly: 57800.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. Jennifer, and just so I’m not mixing terms—when we say gross, we mean before taxes and Medicare and all that. That 57800 is the gross annual income figure you want me using for you, Alex?
Client 1: Yes, gross annual income is 57800.

Advisor: Okay. Jennifer, do you have any accounts in your name we should include in that investable total?
Client 2: I do—there’s an account at a brokerage, basically bank-type money, and it’s 67200.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And then separate from investable accounts, you also have your home. What are you valuing the primary residence at right now?
Client 1: We have it at 171900.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m clear, you’re not carrying any loans against it—no HELOC or anything like that?

Advisor: Great. Alex, just confirming your role in the household—would you say you’re the primary person I should treat as the main point of contact for the planning and the accounts?
Client 1: Yes—primary.
Advisor: Got it. And for both of you, you’re both fully retired at this point, right—no part-time work or anything like that?
Client 2: Right, retired. No work.
Client 1: Retired, yeah.
